Writing
Editorial The Heart of Fort Worth

The Heart of Fort Worth

Many people consider the stockyards to be the heart of Fort Worth. They think of their brown leather cowboy boots, hats, and bootleg jeans. Many will come here, and that is all they will ever see and know Fort Worth as. With Billy Bob’s, The Rodeo, and PR’s, it is easy to see how this is what the city is known for. Little do these people know that if they were to travel fifteen minutes south, they would encounter a street called Magnolia Street , which would challenge their perception of Fort Worth.

Walking down Magnolia Street, you see a row of buildings. Some are buildings that could easily exist on any other street, such as the medical buildings that surround the local businesses. But at the center of the street, Magnolia has become a home for businesses that might not exist otherwise. Local businesses such as Spiral Dinner, an olive green building that sits at a corner on Magnolia. At the front of the building, multi-colored letters spell out the word vegan. An LGBTQ+ flag hangs on the far right window to show that all are welcome.

A block later, you will see a beige building labeled Benitos. The red paint used to show the restaurant’s name is chipping on the sides, as it has been there since the 80s. A green cloth awning sits above the door, and Mexican serapes hang on the inside of the windows.

History of Magnolia Street: Before Magnolia became the beautiful street it is today it used to be a place that kids were warned to stay away from. Gabi Jimenez (owner of MMV) recalls being told this when she was younger. In the 70s and 80s, this part of the city was crumbling down. In what is now known as “Downtown Fort Worth,” a lifestyle of vices flourished. This area was known as Hell’s Half Acre, but referred to as “The Acre” by many locals. Prostitution, suicides, and gambling were at an all-time high, it was a pit of violence. Cowboys knew Fort Worth as a place that encouraged heavy drinking and spending, although dangerous, going into the area was done in search of fun. The city of Fort Worth was trying to revamp this area, so they started implementing new businesses in the area. Slowly this activity left the area, but it did not disappear. This type of lifestyle simply adapted, it trickled down to Magnolia Street. Due to a recession happening in the 1980s, the businesses in the area were abandoned. What once was a street filled with community and laughter became the street you avoided unless you were looking for danger. The hospitals surrounding the street were consistently experiencing robberies making it hard for them to exist in the area. A larger issue arose when the hospitals were having difficulties updating licenses needed to operate; this was due to the economical state of the neighborhood, as the hospitals could not prove that they were aiding the community.

In hopes of preventing an area with large abandoned buildings, the city council decided that they needed to focus on helping this area. Surprisingly, there was a large population willing to help keep Magnolia a safe area. Thus, committees like Near Southside were created.

If you ever wonder how Magnolia street keeps its streets clean, it is because of the Near Southside Committee. For a street that is so people-oriented, one would be surprised as to how neat the street stands. From anywhere you stand on the street, a trash can is visible, making it morally difficult to litter. Standing in the middle of the street after eating a cup of Cookie Crush from MELT Ice Creams on a hot summer day, one might still take the few steps needed to get the ice cream cup into the trash can. It is easy to walk down Magnolia and appreciate the ambiance without thinking much of what it took to craft it. Behind the scenes there are countless of people helping the street thrive. Companies such as Frost Bank, Carter BloodCare, and Dickies are partnered with Near Southside to help build the community.

New Additions to The Street There have been a lot of new additions to the street. It is primarily known for its wide selection of restaurants, but recently there have been two clothing stores, a plant shop, and a skate shop added to the street.

Magnolia started as a side hustle between two sisters, Liz and Gabi Jimenez. Originally MMV, a boutique, started in 2016 and was a collection of vintage pieces. Both sisters spent two years carefully crafting their first collection of items to sell. From estate sales to thrift stores, Liz and Gabi traveled the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex in search of pieces they felt fit their brand. They spent countless hours searching for these items. Their dream was to have a vintage shop. From 2016 to 2019, Modern Meets Vintage was fully vintage. But at the time, the love for vintage clothing was only starting. After years of dedication towards a vintage-based clothing company, Gabi and Liz had to let go of what they thought MMV would be. “Vintage was popular, but not where we wanted it to be, so we kinda introduced our new stuff too… you have to find the right market for it, whenever we started it wasn’t as popular in Fort Worth so we mainly stuck to Dallas,” said Gabi. At the time, MMV only lived online and in pop-up events. The dream of vintage came to a halt, and Gabi and Liz ventured out to new apparel.

After changing the inventory, Modern Meets Vintage opened a small studio on Vickery Blvd. in Fort Worth, TX. It was a small area that had tinted windows and a black awning hanging outside of it. This is very different from what MMV’s location looks like now, as the Magnolia location thrives on glitter and all things girly. When you first walk in, it is as if you have entered a secret passage into the CandyLand board game. In the fully pink room, three disco balls hang alongside iridescent chandeliers crafted from cellophane. Every corner has something colorful you haven’t seen before. The moment you walk in, you are invited to think outside the box and let your creativity live outside of what we have constructed reality to be. Magnolia has allowed MMV to display its individuality that other locations did not allow.

Individuality is something that is celebrated in a place such as Magnolia, from how the buildings look to how the people dress walking along the street.

As I am listening to Gabi describe how she loves Magnolia Street, and hear her praise its love for self-expression, I hear her refer to it as a “mini Austin,” which catches me by surprise, but I somewhat agree with it. As I look around, I can see exactly what she is talking about. A man walks by, holding a newly purchased record. A green plastic earring hangs on his left ear, he is wearing black cargo pants that are stitched by zippers, and through his black mesh shirt, a moon tattoo is peeking out. The man walks around confidently, although he is wearing something that many may find unconventional or “out there,” he is unaware of this; as Magnolia has created a safe place for him. This once “out there” outfit is now an outfit he wears comfortably as if it were his own skin.

There is a safety bubble that surrounds the street of Magnolia, a bubble that makes you feel that clothing can be a form of expression. Nevertheless, Magnolia is a place for everyone, some may choose to outwardly express themselves through their style, but not all choose to do so. The main attraction of this part of the city is that it invites you to be whoever you choose to be.

ArtsGoggle

There is one day of the year that has Magnolia filled with over 60,000 people. One singular event causes the streets of Magnolia to be flooded with individuals searching for a good time. The event is ArtsGoggle. The event has been going on for about twenty years. It all started as a gallery night that was hosted in hopes of displaying local art, and it continued this way until the event grew so large that it leaked over onto the street. ArtsGoggle now covers 21 blocks, and will most likely grow as the years go on. Artists booths and vendor tents fill the streets black concrete. There may be a booth that sells a variety of crystals to your left and a booth filled with handcrafted bedazzled sunglasses to your right. There is no telling what you may stumble across as you make your way down the booths, but it will be nothing short from unique.

“I think they’ve really created a place where people can showcase their art,” said Sandra Falcon, the co-owner of Luna Antigua. Sandra has been able to work ArtsGoggle through her clothing company Luna Antigua and her business Falcon Photos. She first attended this event in 2014 with her photography business which at the time was called House of Boston. A big white plastic photo booth sat in front of BREWED. At first, it was slow, as people were just getting to the event, but before they knew it, the photo booth had a line that reached the other vendors. Children and adults were so excited to pick up props they could use in their pictures. The props ranged from a mustache to excessively large pink glasses, Sandra and her husband Chris Falcon had one goal in mind when creating the photo booth; to capture people having fun. Images of silly faces and smiles were the only thing that the photo booth captured that night. Sandra’s brown eyes light up as she recalls her first night in ArtsGoggle. “We booked a lot of jobs because of that night, even BREWED hired us for their Christmas party later that year.” Sandra continues to explain that because the prices for a booth are relatively cheap, the businesses filling the streets tend to be more local to the area. She compares the prices to the Main Street art festival that takes place in Downtown Fort Worth, and they are significantly higher, which is why more established artists tend to be the ones occupying their booths. “At ArtsGoggle there are people who do art as a hobby and want to showcase it, as well as established artists who do art as a living.” ArtsGoggle does a good job of making every artist and vendor feel comfortable showing their art, everyone’s displays are mixed around, meaning that there are no sections. The whole point of ArtsGoggle is to explore art that is unknown.

In 2020, Sandra Falcon got to work ArtsGoggle through Etico, a store that carried different women-led businesses. “It was a really special moment for me,” says Sandra. That day Sandra’s parents came to the store at 6:00 AM and helped her clean and set up the entire store for the big day. They spent hours making sure that everything was perfect as they wanted to help alleviate any stress their daughter may have. Luna Antigua In the Luna Antigua section of the store gold clothing racks held a rainbow of women and children's clothing. Each piece was hand-loomed in Guatemala and on the tag of the item laid the Luna Antigua logo. The logo was inspired by traditional Mayan drawings and has a crescent moon in the center, right under the moon there are three symbols that signal completion. Without knowing it, Luna Antigua had referenced Mayan culture, as the moon goddess was considered the first weaver. When Sandra found this out she felt that it fit perfectly with their brand, as Luna Antigua was made to showcase the tradition of weaving in a way that honored it. Sandra stated that while the pieces were still very true to traditional pieces, they were slightly altered to keep up with current trends. Since weaving is a dying art, Luna Antigua was determined to show the beauty in this skill. Etico once stood at the end of Magnolia street, pink ombre flower garlands used to decorate the windows. If you look up Etico on the internet today, a big red tab will read “permanently closed” right under it. On Google maps, the image of what once was Etico is now MMV.

When asked about the end of Etico Sandra states, “[Magnolia] was good for Etico, but it was not right for Luna Antigua.” According to Sandra, most of Luna Antigua’s clientele resides near Vickery. Since Luna Antigua’s pieces are described as more “traditional,” it would make sense that pieces like this did not do as well in an area such as Magnolia, where a store that is more “young” and “trendy” like MMV did.

Although Magnolia seems to be ever-changing, its development does not seem to be ending any time soon. New restaurants and events will continue to appear on Magnolia, keeping its trendy and hipster vibe. It seems that events such as ArtsGoggle have gathered a lot of attention for the area, and this area will only grow with time. This year [2024], Magnolia reopened its doors to the OpenDoor event; similar to ArtsGoggle, this event is meant to bring the community together through the arts. This event has been closed since 2019, due to COVID, but returned in April. So now there are two events that will flood the streets of Magnolia. Whether you are in search of a new favorite coffee shop, a new outfit, or a new plant, you should definitely check out one of these events.

Editorial Essay The Clean Girl, Her Rise and Fall

Zoe-Kylie Sanchez Spring 2023 Dr. Leverenz

The Clean Girl, Her Rise and Fall and The Unveiling of The Mob Wife

As we all know, COVID-19 was still on the rise in 2022 which caused many to reflect on their health choices. While the world was in a chaotic rut, many were hoping to prevent the devastating effects of COVID-19. The solution; was a healthier lifestyle. This bleed bled into the beauty community. The biggest luxury during COVID was our health; both physical and mental. Beauty brands were aware of this so they marketed a new lifestyle; thus the "Clean Girl"arrived at the scene. This was a stark contrast to the highly saturated indie aesthetic that we saw at the beginning of COVID-19, which personally took over my friend group. Going through COVID-19 at the age of fifteen makes you search for anything that brings you joy at the time– that means vibrant colors. So you can imagine how I felt when my For You page began to be filled with outfits that consisted of a white t-shirt and jeans. As a seventeen year old I was unimpressed. As I have gotten older I have grown to understand the aesthetic of The Clean Girl. The search for a calm and organized life where a stable routine is implemented sounds like heaven, but alas it is currently just a dream. -​ - - She wakes up at 6:00 AM with glowy dewy skin. The first thing she does in the morning is reach for her five-minute journal-- a journal meant to remind you of all the things you should be grateful for. Then she goes to a Pilates class. After that, she will grab a green smoothie. She is known as the Clean Girl. If you're still having difficulty picturing her, that’s because this lifestyle feels unachievable, but to help you understand, you should scroll over to the skincare brand Rhode’s on Instagram. The face of the clean girl aesthetic is no other than Hailey Bieber; who has carefully curated the image of a minimal makeup look that highlights beautiful, healthy, and glass skin. Everyone in 2022 was striving for this look and lifestyle.; Although it is now dying, this trend still lives on in 2024. Her wardrobe consists of slick back hairstyles and gold hoops, recently she has added a darker lipliner paired with lipgloss. Although many may not know this; as they put this new aesthetic on, they gave room for the issue of cultural appropriation. Although I understand the appeal of the aesthetic, I also see the undeniable controversy. The aesthetic has been said to steal from women of color. Whenever women of color were wearing these things they were seen as “ghetto” but now that Anglo women wear them they are seen as attractive. This claim is true. The reason that Hispanic women began wearing a darker lipliner was that the beauty industry was not making lip liners that matched their skin tone, so they were innovative and started using brown eyebrow products. Oddly, the origins of this trend stem from excluding different skin tones. The ignorance surrounding the cultural appropriation within aesthetics like the "Clean Girl" show us deeply rooted ethical issues. Ignoring the issues within these aesthetics allows for these systemic inequalities to continue; as they also allow harmful stereotypes to continue. In the context of the "Clean Girl" aesthetic, elements traditionally associated with communities of color, such as hairstyles, makeup techniques, and fashion choices, are selectively adopted and mainstreamed by white influencers without acknowledgment of their cultural significance. In this day and age, we have seen similar situations happen on social media. In 2020 (at the beginning of COVID-19) the TikTok dance renegade was extremely popular. (To this day my family still makes fun of me because they remember me trying to learn the dance.) This dance became popular because of the TikToker Charli D’amelio. Whenever anyone thinks of the dance, Charli D’amelio is immediately thought of; the crazy thing about this situation is that Charli D’amelio took this dance from another content creator: Jaliah Harmon. Many of us now know that this is the case as Charli D’amelio later credited her after receiving backlash, but no one is able to name Jaliah Harmon from memory alone. Nevertheless, I think that at the end of the day, I do not have a problem with this style becoming popular, but I do have a problem with others not knowing where these trends stemmed from. These stylistic choices were not described as “clean” when a woman of color chose to wear them, so the underlying racism is very prevalent. Not knowing that these trends were once looked down upon because they were primarily worn by women of color, will cause the cycle to continue. Awareness could help broaden our perspective on beauty. Like all trends, they must come to an end, and the number one suspect for ending the Clean Girl is the Mob Wife. Both women live a life of luxury, the difference is that the clean girl is focused on a holistic way of life, while the Mob Wife would rather live in extravagance (at least that is what social media has portrayed her) The “Mob Wife” lives a life very different from the “Clean Girl”. She wears what the “Clean Girl” would never dare to wear. With a leopard-printed fur coat, dark red lipstick, and leather pants, she is meant to symbolize confidence and wealth, but once again we run into an issue with the aesthetic. The aesthetic glorifies a life that is full of harsh violence. Oftentimes the mob wife seen on television is seen as powerful, but this is not the case as they are often stuck in unhealthy loops. For example: Elvira Hancock in the movie Scarface. Elvira is in an abusive relationship with the character Tony Montana, she is addicted to cocaine and is constantly exposed to gruesome violence due to her relationship. This storyline can easily be dismissed because of the wealth and beauty the character Elvira possesses; this is not a story to be idealized, it is a sad one. I am sure that this aesthetic will be soon replaced by another aesthetic that glorifies a group that is oppressed, or soon it will be replaced by one that does not credit an oppressed group. Currently, the fashion and beauty world is being flooded with new aesthetics in the name of helping others explore their style, but in reality, these aesthetics are creating unrealistic narratives that can be harmful. It is easy to get caught up in the beauty that is portrayed in these aesthetics, but the truth is, that many of these trends fail to display the pain that comes along with the narratives attached to the style choices.

Poetry Out of Season
Out of Season

On that warm summer day,
at the farmer’s market,
I held your left hand;
and with the other,
you handed me an apricot.
Its skin was surrounded with
fuzz. An odd texture,
that reminded me of petting a hairless cat.
So instead I reached for a pear;
causing your fingers to slip out of my grasp.

“A pear in July?”

I offered you a slice,
and was met with a monologue of your hatred of pears;
calling it the most disappointing pome fruit,
You criticized its shape and color,
“a weak impersonation of an apple”

So I picked up the apricot and cut it to share.
giving you three slices and keeping one, and
Forced myself to enjoy its goo
Forced myself to love
the mushy substance.

Then the trees began to lose their leaves
and the apricots went with them.
I guess I should be happy
that October has surrounded me with pears,
but the only person to share with
is a green-eyed stranger.

Now when I hold the stranger’s hand
at the farmer’s market,
I search for you in the crowd, hoping
that amongst all piles of pears,
an extra apricot is hidden.
 
Short Fiction Confessions of The Self-Medicated

Confessions of The Self-Medicated A box of bandaids sits on my bedside table, in case of an emergency. This morning I placed one over a bruise on my left knee. Yesterday I placed a bandaid on the roof of my tongue, around my first molar, and against the gum of my teeth, I had a toothache. Tomorrow I think I will place one on my upper lip, in case I have a nosebleed. If you look at what lies next to my bandaid box you’ll see five bottle caps scattered around, and five beer bottles lined up against the wall. These are my bandaids for the wounds you can not see when the weight of my breath has weighed me down and glued me to the sheets of my bed. I lay there, hoping the alcohol would cleanse the wounds inside me, hoping it will remove the bacteria, and keep my wounds from becoming infected. I saw my father tend his wounds this way, his disease of constant self-pity stamped him with depression. I remember watching him with a straw-colored liquid stain on his shirt around his chest, and he would let it sit there as if it was seeping through his skin, into his heart, and cleansing it. I didn’t understand why my father reached for this liquid, but I assumed it had some magical property since it could turn my father from a weak man to a brute. When I was older I found out that this was not true, but that adulthood was filled with bills and disappointments, a reality I could heal through a bottle of intoxicating liquid. You may wonder how restoration has been found in something so destructive. I have been trained this way. When a zit emerges from under my skin I use a face wash to help it heal, and it burns, but that’s what makes me think it is working. Similar to the way that beer burns my insides. Raised with the idea that pain is the result of functionality. Now there are many reasons to avoid this method of medication. Some side effects are that my lips are often chapped, and my mind runs a little foggy. For that, I am truly sorry. I am not always present. I do not collect memories, I collect a collection of forgotten moments. I exist in only fragments. 24 years diminished to 17. Today I woke up with eight new bruises, they are all scattered across my body. Don’t ask me how they got there. The “how” is not very relevant, they are simply there; If I were to dwell on it I fear that I would enter a perpetual loop of curiosity and wonder. I can not afford such luxury. If I am honest I have accepted the fact that a bruise will always remain on my body. At this point, I have started to believe that my skin is beginning to shed its melanin and acquired a taste for green and purple pigmentation. Although my body has decided that my wounds should be visible, I do my best to conceal the byproduct of my hurt; mending my skin with bandaids. The tan adhesive merges with my skin and the wounds of a blackout no longer exist.

​

As I sat in the chapel I was surrounded by nothing but the sound of my own thoughts, and that is when I realized what Hell was like. Hell is not the place where your skin burns for eternity, but the overwhelming thoughts of being a failure and

If it was sour I was immune.

A box of bandaids sits on my bedside table, in case of an emergency. This morning I placed one over a bruise on my left knee. Yesterday I placed a bandaid on the roof of my tongue, around my first molar, and against the gum of my teeth, I had a toothache. Tomorrow I think I will place one on my upper lip, in case I have a nosebleed. If you look at what lies next to my bandaid box you’ll see five bottle caps scattered around, and five beer bottles lined up against the wall. These are my bandaids for the wounds you can not see, when the weight of my breath has weighed me down and glued me to the sheets of my bed. I lay there, hoping the alcohol will cleanse the wounds inside me, hoping it would remove the bacteria, and keep my wounds from becoming infected. I saw my father tend his wounds this way, the straw-colored liquid would stain his shirt around his chest, and he would let it sit there as if it was seeping through his skin, into his heart, and cleansing it. I didn’t understand why my father reached for this liquid, but i assumed it had some magical property since it could turn my father from a weak man to a brute. When I was older I found out that this was not true, and decided, this is just what adults do. You may wonder how I find restoration in something so destructive. I have been trained this way. When a zit emerges from under my skin I use a face wash to help it heal, and it burns, but that’s what makes me think it is working. Similar to the way that beer burns my insides. Raised with the idea that pain is the result of labor. Now there are many reasons to avoid this method of medication. Some side effects are that my lips are often chapped, and my mind runs a little foggy. For that, I am truly sorry. I am not always present. I do not collect memories I collect a collection of forgotten moments. I exist in only fragments. 24 years diminished to 17. Today I woke up with eight new bruises, they are all scattered across my body. Don’t ask me how they got there. The “how” is not very relevant, they are simply there; If I were to dwell on it I fear that I would enter a perpetual loop of curiosity and wonder. I can not afford such luxury. If I am honest I have accepted the fact that a bruise will always remain on my body. At this point, I have started to believe that my skin is beginning to shed its melanin and acquired a taste for green and purple pigmentation. Although my body has decided that my wounds should be visible, I do my best of trying to conceal the byproduct of my hurt; mending my skin with bandaids. The tan adhesive merges with my skin and it is as if nothing ever happened. After the daily routine, I go downstairs, Immediately I hear the cries of a two-year-old. The sound is unbearable, the sound echoes in my ear as if they have captured the sound and decided to elongate it’s stay. As I sit down at the table my wife brings out a plate of black beans and slaps a tortilla on the wooden table. Add argument or conflict with mother, mom not trusting father taking care of Ramona. “Eat” she says So I do. She moves around the kitchen trying to soothe the baby, and she sings to it. She’s singing a song that used to come on a television show when we were younger. “Younger” as if I am not still a child myself. I guess my medication has eaten the cells needed to mature. Eventually, the baby stops filling the house with its resentful scream. Now I can rest, maybe even tend my wounds. I get up from the table and go towards the couch. My body begs me to go to sleep, my head is still pounding and it feels as if my ears are beginning to throb. I am not sure whether these are side effects of my disease or my medication. I suppose they are indistinguishable now, both have made me ill and both are fatal. As I dose off I start to think of what I will have for dinner (I shouldn’t take my medication on empty stomach). Somehow the thought of food triggered a dream where I was feeding my baby girl a bottle. It was when she was only a couple of weeks old; her head rested on my hand, while her body lay on my arm. She was fragile, and I could see how the warm milk was soothing her to sleep. She knew she was safe, I was keeping her safe. When I woke up I went into the room next to mine. A bright light startled me as I entered, then slowly everything in the room got into focus. There she was playing with a plastic stethoscope. She smiles when she sees me standing at the door. “Sit” she says So I do. I sit next to her on the beige carpet and she places the stethoscope on my left knee over the bandaid. “Healed” she says So I am. I decide that my medication won’t be necessary today. Instead, I share some apple juice with my daughter. I can feel the fruity taste linger for minutes after I have had it. I haven’t had apple juice since I was little, the taste is nostalgic, it reminds me of the times my father would take me apple picking. He would place me on his shoulders and I would reach for the shiniest apple on the tree. Suddenly, the bandaids begin to fall off. I begin to recall memories that I have not been able to or rather refused to. I expect my bruises to become visible, but they are gone. I keep drinking the apple juice until there is nothing left. I hug my daughter, I know she does not understand what is going on but she is a healer. Apple juice, and alcohol, the daughter gets him a beer “How did you do it, Ramona?” All she does is smile in response. She is only two years old but I have been worried that my disease would torment her as it did me. I feared that when she grew older she would see the way I medicated and resent me for it, or worse imitate my actions. It sounds hypocritical to tell someone that they can’t live the way you live, but I wanted her to be better than I; I know she will do better. Ramona is a piece of sunshine, and I will not let the world dim her light, nor will I let myself. Once again I will become her support system, when she is older she will not need to worry about me as her mother does. I know this because I am getting better, I no longer need my medication. I won’t lie, sometimes I feel the urge to reach for it but I am trying my best. I know that one day I will get better. I will get better for my Ramona. I will keep my disease from infecting her. I might not be able to provide her much of the material world but I will create a safe place. I want her home to feel like home and not just the place she sleeps in at night. Later that night I bring a warm cup of chamomile tea to the bedroom. My wife is laying on the bed watching television, I walk in and hand her the mug. A puzzled look takes over her facial expressions. “ I am sorry, I am getting better” I say She reaches for my head and nods, as if she is letting the words sink in. I can tell that she is weary to believe me, I have said those words to her before. This time we both understand that I mean it. The television continues to play in the background as we talk about the past years of our marriage. She is careful in how she phrases her words as if I am fragile and her words could shatter me. I simply listen to what she has to say, I accept that I have not been there for her or Ramona. After a long conversation, she lays her head on the pillow and falls asleep. I have never seen her look so peaceful, usually, I am the one to go to sleep first and wake up last. This time she will rest. While she rests, I decide that I will move the box of bandaids into the medicine cabinet in the restroom. I won’t be needing them any time soon. As I get into bed I start to think of breakfast ( I shouldn’t teach Ramona to skip breakfast). I think I should make chocolate chip pancakes for the three of us tomorrow.

I sat in the corner of the coffee shop staring blankly at the wall behind her, there she was with ramona, but I was alone. “My symptoms are my pain” Acting a little wired “Too intoxicated to be scared”

Eclectic Western Bedroom

A rich, moody bedroom concept combining emerald velvet, deep burgundy linens, antler accents, and artisan textiles — western heritage with a maximalist, collected-over-time sensibility.
